speaker: interviewer
text: This is Hanna Lifschutz. Hanna, we talked at the last tape, at the end of the last-- first tape about your brother developing tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewee
text: --tuberculosis. Well, I tell it briefly although it is a-- it is a very painful and forever ever memorable time of my life. Anyway, I used to go out. At this time was still possible to go out of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but you were not allowed. You were actually not-- but I don't know why-- they were quite friendly, the Germans allowed you to go through the gate. But if outside the gate, there were Polish boys very often or people. And if they found you, they'd say, Jew, Jew, Jew, Jew, and they took you to the Polish-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Police.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the, to the police. And especially you were noticeable because you were not allowed in the winter to wear any fur or anything, you know. But that was-- at this time was already closed, the ghetto. And-- but nonetheless, I used to take this-- we didn't have a yellow badge. We had white and blue I think it was. And we had already the Jewish policemen who were guarding and had to help you know with the keeping order in the ghetto. I mentioned-- I didn't mention before, they were already formed, this police, Jewish police. And they had to keep order with, with, with everlasting, increasing population in the ghetto, which swell afterwards to I think it was 3-- 300-- 300-- 500-- 5,000-- over 300,000 Jews. Some people say 400,000, some say 300,000. Anyway, 300,000 perished. And I used to go to, to Otwock and see my brother. And then on one special occasion-- because I don't want to make it long, he already was very, very ill. And he seemed like he was waiting for me to come to die in presence of his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old he was at the time?

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was, he was-- how old was he?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he must have been 20. And I came to-- and the nurses told me already. And we spoke the whole night. And he was very cheerful and he said, do I hurt? There is a, a sunny, a sunny cemetery in Otwock. Do bury me there on the sunny side. And it was very traumatic, of course. And he died the same night. And I had the task to, to see to, to for burial. And I enlisted the, the, the nurses, the kindly nurse. Enlisted the carpenter, carpenter who made a coffin, a wooden coffin. And we put the coffin on a cart, with a horse. And I was the only one, so the, the, the, the coffin was on the one-horse cart. And the carpenter was sitting on that or standing. I was walking behind the coffin. And it was a very nice, sunny day, very sunny day, May. And the lane-- we made the same journey with my daughter and my son-in-law, which we found by a miracle. And we went to a little Jewish cemetery. And the little-- the carpenter helped me to dig the grave. And we put the coffin to--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nobody else but you and the carpenter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. There was nobody. My parents were with the ghetto. My parents were in the ghetto. And I left the ghetto, taking off my [INAUDIBLE] and went to-- so I was the only one. And then we made, we buried the coffin. And we put a lot of furs with the hands and he had the shovel-- he had a spade. And then I gathered a lot of summer flowers from the vicinity and covered the whole grave with the flowers. And put a name on it, which there was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you use to put the name on?

speaker: interviewee
text: A piece of wood. I put-- didn't put-- I put a-- It was no, no, nothing to be-- they put a stone. I put a stone and a piece of wood. And said a prayer. I said shema and, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever I remember, you know, our great, our great, the only Jewish, you know, verse that almost everybody know, every Jewish person knows. And didn't even finish. Looked up to the sky and left it. You know. And we, in 1940-- 1995, my daughter and my son-in-law, we found this place. We went to the same lane and the men who took us by car-- was a small line-- I have the photographs-- which was a Jewish cemetery in Polish written. And we follow that. And there were two cemeteries. One was Christian. I recognized the lane. It was endless walk, for hours, you know, walking. Going by car, it was far enough, but walking. I was walking after the coffin endless. But it was sunny. And then I left my brother there, you know. And we [INAUDIBLE]. And my son-in-law, Peter-- and Peter said kaddish at this, at one of the graves that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: After more than 50 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of my-- after more than 50 years, we found it. We didn't find the grave, but it must have been somewhere there. So we said kaddish on one of these. And that was-- then I, I didn't-- I was returning, was evening, and I was returning to the ghetto. It was a very long way because-- from Otwock to take the electric train, everything was a danger to go. But because I didn't look Jewish, that was my only luck. I didn't look Jewish, and I didn't speak Polish with an accent, so it was my only luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know how I reached the ghetto, what I was going to say to my mother, to my stepmother. I mean, I was devastated and horrified. How am I going to tell this poor woman that her beloved, beloved son died and other one was in the big ghetto? And she was left with my father and with me, and I was her stepdaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a terrible task. It was a traumatic experience. Anyway, I reached the ghetto. And then we lived in the ghetto. You know, I don't know what you want to ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in the ghetto, you know, we had a very spacious and very nice flat. Not a luxury flat, but a very nice flat and comfortable. And we were cramped. I can't tell you how many people were put. We had only one room to ourselves. We, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say we, it's your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my father, my stepmother, I, and the beginning my little brother. And then I was happy to, to to, to manage to get him into a shop in the big ghetto, because otherwise if he was out of work was dangerous. He wouldn't be able to live there. Lots of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in the ghetto, how shall I put it? We were cramped, as I told you. Oh, forever more refugees, and more refugees, and more. And they-- every single place was taken. The children-- as this time progressed from 1940 to 1942, children were on the street dying everywhere. Masses of people were dying of pure starvation. They were swollen, and they were left on the street. The children were begging and died there where they were. They were-- we all eventually sold all our possessions, all, you know, the silverware. Who was buying? They were coming to the ghetto. They were coming. Christian people or other people, they were coming. And you could, through intermediaries somehow, you could sell. Everything was being sold-- odd jobs in stitching, odd jobs in cleaning, odd jobs in, in washing. I don't know what. Whatever was possible just to bring a little bread home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, I do remember so vividly my poor Rosa, my poor stepmother, she-- horse meat sometimes she cooked, rotten vegetables, or good veg-- one soup a day. That was all we could have. And the soup was watery. And the bread was hardly any substantial bread. We really starved. And I used to even give in the beginning-- my little brother was with me. I used to give it to him because my, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] my heart was breaking for then. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] It's difficult. Even after all these years, it's difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, somehow, somehow even in this, even in this very profound and very, very hard times, we young people from, you know, France and different places, we used to meet, close all windows and cover the windows. And we met together. And we read. People who could read poetry and people who could play guitar [DRINKS] in very subdued tones. And we had sometimes a very charming evenings. And everybody saved something and put on the table. And we had it all together, you know. And maybe somebody somehow saved some little wine from golden old times. So we had this evening, young ones. And even there were people, you know, who taught, people, very well-educated people, who had taught young people history, history of Jewish people. And I know very well that there were-- it was-- he was a lawyer, Ringelblum, Ringelblum.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr.-- he was-- I think then his name was Ringelblum. I think so. I talked about him and I heard about him again in the Polish Historical Institute in Warsaw, which I visited in 1995. And he recorded the events of the ghetto very, very carefully. And he put it in three enormous milk tanks, which they-- no, milk containers. And they did it like, you know, like in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Smuggled?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the, in the scrolls in, in, in Palestine those days. And he put it in this, and it was buried. And all the evidence to after the war were found, and they are in the Polish Jewish Historical Institute in Warsaw in safekeeping, which they were found. The third was not found. And the ghetto life was recorded. And so it's all true. And they keep all the evidence there in this Institute where I visited. And actually, which I deposited my book, which I have written about all the events. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: These studies were held in Polish or in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They were in Yiddish, they were in Polish. They were sporadic. They were not, you know, they were not consistent. But a certain culture in spite of this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Horrible--

speaker: interviewee
text: --absolute disaster and tragedy, it was still a spark of Jewish cultural life. And it's unfortunate there were no schools anymore. Those people who didn't manage to finish their school, they didn't. And it was a very hard, a very hard life in this ghetto. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What age you were? At the time, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1920. How old was I-- in 1920, so-- this is very easy--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was about 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no work.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a pure starvation. I used to go to bed-- we all used to go to bed starving, starving absolute. And my stepmother and stepfather tried with all the selling-- we had quite a lot of-- you know the Jewish homes? A lot of silver, a lot of golden pieces, and everything. So we maintained ourself barely by this soup and bread. And then in '41, we were told we are going to move into the Tobbens' shops. The premises-- that what I told you was a general ghetto view, because the people were coming, and amassing, and trying to, to, to, to find place. But, yes, I forgot. My father, my father has been infected with typhoid. Typhoid was very rampant. And there were no hospital except one, [NON-ENGLISH] Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto? Or outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the ghetto. It was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And but there were-- can you imagine? It was overcrowded. So the authorities said that those who have typhoid, only one person can look after the ill patient. And so my stepmother-- at this time, we still was ghetto. It was not yet the Tobbens' shop. So they had to go to neighbors. And my mother has a cousin-- had a cousin-- very friendly with her. She was a very good dancer. They went to them. And I looked after my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, you know, you had to boil all the clothes and sweating. And he didn't eat anything. And occasionally a nurse came to look after-- to see him. You know, a Jewish nurse came to see what he was doing. I mean, what was the cure for typhoid, this type of typhoid? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was infection from a, from a louse, you know? It's an infection from-- people were full of these vermins. But we kept it clean as much as we can. And it was very clean, even in these circumstances with other people who we lived. And the other people had also to move out, so it was only I with my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I actually wish that he should die because it was, it was-- we knew that somehow, somehow or other there is something not quite right. But he-- I saved him because I cleaned the clothes. And what was the saver? Because he was starved. In typhoid if you don't eat, then you survive. And he survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And shortly afterwards, we were called that we have to go to the Tobbens Schultz premises. And I was the-- at my-- at the time, just at this time, I managed my brother to, to, to, to, to get him to the big ghetto. And I went-- and actually if I may tell you that I went myself with him there and crossed the no man's land, which was very dangerous because already at the time the Germans begin to select people for selection and took them at random straight to Treblinka. We didn't know yet that. But I managed to take him to this place, to this shop where he was with the electric workshop. I went to the place. I found the place with my grandmother-- my grandfather was not alive anymore-- and my auntie, and her son, and her husband used to live. It was a beautiful flat, big flat with, with, with, with a balcony and flowers on it, and-- in Nowolipki Street. And it was also extraordinary experience. I found the place, but was a complete-- I forgot to tell you that the-- already in '41, they have, the Germans began the selection. Slowly, but they began the-- not slowly, rather, rather quite consistently.

speaker: interviewer
text: What really happened during the selections? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I will come to it. I'll just finished with my auntie. So I went, wanted to find what is with my family going, and my auntie was the first stop. And I entered the house. It was completely silent. She lived in a flat. There were about seven, six flats or eight flats, a big house. Was complete silence. And I went-- there were no lifts, so I walked to the stairs. Silence. Every door was open. No one. Not a person there. I went up to my auntie's place. The door, it was not locked but it was not open. I pushed it and I found the familiar place that we knew, I knew very well. And I screamed, Auntie! Auntie! And then I heard very fine, very faint noise from under the-- there were two enormous cupboards, drawers-- not drawers-- wardrobes, enormous wardrobes. You know, like in the olden days. And from behind the wardrobes I heard like a faint squealing noise. And there was sitting my poor grandmother on a wheelchair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Auntie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wheeled her out, and she told me the whole-- you know, you know it was a traumatic embracing, and crying, and weeping, and asking questions. And she told me that my auntie just the day before, she was-- the Germans came-- which I'll tell you afterwards how they conducted the selections-- and they called everybody down. And she went down with her son, and the husband came. Before she went down, she hid my grandmother behind the cupboards. I mean, just thinking she's going to save her. And there was another auntie who lived there, but she was not at home when the selection took place. So she used to come in the evenings still and feed my grandmother. In the day she kept her behind the cupboard. She probably was shot dead afterwards by Germans. And my uncle who was my auntie's husband, came home a day before and saw that his wife was not there, so he give himself up to the Germans immediately. I slept overnight there. My brother was deposited to this place. And I took him. And then I had to leave my aunt-- another aunt of mine came in the evening. And she brought-- she worked in one of the shops still, so she brought some little food, some, I don't know, kind of vegetables. She made again a little soup with some bread. And we all ate it. I slept overnight and wept all the time nonstop. And then left my-- said goodbye to my beloved grandmother and to this aunt and went back to my small ghetto, where I still had my father and my stepmother. And that I was not taken by crossing the no man's land and taken by the Germans was just an accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you asked me how the Germans conducted the-- Selections.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the selections. About my personal selection, I'll tell you later. But the selections, there usually came a few armored cars with armed soldiers, rifles with the foreboding-looking uniform, Prussian, and with the green uniforms, and the big boots, and the officers with the leather coats, fabulously dressed. And sometimes they had a dog with them. And young people, very young. They came to every house and shouted, 'rous!' 'Rous! Rous! Rous!' Means come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish policemen had to help them. So the Jewish policemen, they went up and down and searched in the-- you know, being too ardent in wanting to help. And they were forced also to help. And, and, and so the people took little bundles. All they would take, were allowed to take, was a little suitcase and a bundle. And the children took the bundles with the parents. And you have seen. It's all well known, and well recorded, and well photographed that how they looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a very forbidding and very, very traumatic. And not only traumatic, it was a very frightening thing, when they called 'rous' and went to the flat and said 'rous.' And then if somebody didn't go, shot him dead. So everybody ran like, like, like sheep. You see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who shot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to-- shot them? The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I wanted to tell you because after the war, my first trip in 1948, it was when I went to Israel, my first trip to Israel with my late husband, and we kissed the Earth. And at some point we were encountered or we were met by some people from-- kibbutzim from which we visited. And we were asked, how come that the Jewish people of Poland let themselves go like sheep without forming any resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the young Sabras couldn't understand it. They said, galut Jews. They said, we despise them. How can they be so cowardly? How could they do things like that? How could they not defend themselves? Resist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will tell you. The Jewish people of Poland were very strictly Orthodox people, majority. I mean, they were very highly educated class. And they were lawyers, and doctors, and surgeons, and philosophers, and professors. It was a big part of Polish culture, Polish Jewish culture. And they were a secular and [INAUDIBLE]. But predominantly, the religious Jew was taught to follow the law of our, of our God Almighty. He didn't have guns. He was not told how to resist. He didn't know how to defend himself. If he was told that he has to go for resettlement, what the Germans said. That was resettlement, the verse which was used. They went. They didn't know. And they were so perfidious the Germans. They were so perfidious, because they each time did tell you, oh, you are going to the next place, and you will be resettled. You will work on the land or you will work in factories.

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- we didn't have the idea yet there was Treblinka. Who could perceive such a inhuman situation as the cultural and highly educated and the such musical German nation? Who'd invent for the Jewish, for the human beings like themselves? So the Jewish people of Poland didn't know how to defend themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Sabras later on, you see, when the Israeli people unfortunately found out, you know, of the hijacking of the plane, they perhaps had an example how you can be terrorized by two people with guns. And you can be 100 or 300 people in the jumbo jet and you are terrorized by three guns.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were-- the Jewish people were terrorized. They were not cowards. They were not cowards. They were victims of circumstances and believers in God Almighty and their own religion. But they were never cowards. So-- So you were talking about the selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So then-- so that was the general selection. So they did select very systematically, from house to house. From house to house they went and they took people to a place called in, in also in the north ghetto. The place was called Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- and I know we shall visit it again in 19-- after 50 years. I couldn't put my foot on, on, on Polish soil, but I was persuaded by my delightful son-in-law Peter and my darling daughter who went with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there was an enormous, vast, empty place, which they emptied. It was near a railway. The railway took to nearby places from this Umschlagplatz. Finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, and Treblinka was a very small town near Warsaw. It was not like Auschwitz, which was near Crakow. Warsaw people mostly, and people who were brought to Warsaw from various provinces, who have consisted of this nearly 400,000 people or 350,000. They one day-- the Umschlagplatz was without any facilities-- no drinking facility, no toilet facilities, no any human facilities at all. And the trains were nearby, so they were loaded. They were taken by German police from all the streets of the ghetto, amassed in Umschlagplatz, taken into the wagons, slammed in like animals, locked in properly, and taken to Treblinka. And then the journey took sometimes few days, because the Germans in Treblinka didn't manage to gas so many people or to shoot so many people in the beginning because that famous Zyklon gas was invented later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were sometimes cramped-- and they're crammed like you have seen in Spielberg film. In the Shoah he showed it how it was so I won't dwell upon it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were given orders to move then in '41 to the Tobbens Schultz place.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're coming now to the end of the second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
